directions.name: Full-width PTX fragment-issue scheduling with tighter live ranges
directions.hypothesis: The restored accepted PTX base is still winning because orchestration and paired export already worked, but the remaining shape is clear: barrier and mio are low, occupancy is still 1, and long scoreboard is now the more visible residual stall. The best long-run next step is a full-width 64x384 PTX dataflow follow-through that does not reopen the regressed half-panel compute split and does not panelize B-load reorder. Instead, flatten the hot-band fragment issue order so each B fragment is loaded and consumed in a tighter scope inside the existing 12-tile kernel, reducing compiler-visible live ranges without changing the outer tile or the 64x96 tail.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual long-scoreboard latency and register-limited live state inside the current full-width PTX helper surface are now more limiting than synchronization overhead.
directions.risk: This is still PTX-local but not trivial: changing the full-width issue order can increase instruction count or accidentally drift toward the already-losing half-panel explicit mma.sync path. The payoff may also be modest if the compiler already keeps the fragment lifetimes tighter than expected.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if long-scoreboard and registers do not both move in the right direction, or if the implementation starts recreating the previously regressed half-panel compute path.

directions.name: PTX state and helper lifetime compaction without pair-compaction
directions.hypothesis: A safer but lower-ceiling continuation is to keep the current full-width PTX compute, fixed-K orchestration retime, and paired export exactly as they are, but reduce register pressure by compacting helper structure and template recursion around the 12-tile accumulator set. The target is not the prechecked pair-compaction experiment; it is the remaining compiler-visible lifetime inflation in PtxWmmaAccTileSet384, ptx_wmma_acc_tile(), ptx_wmma_accumulate_tile_set_384(), and the paired export helpers, which may still be enough to hold the kernel at 167 registers/thread.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Helper-induced live-range inflation around the named 12-tile PTX accumulator surface is still contributing to occupancy_limit_registers=1 even after the productive orchestration/export work.
directions.risk: This is the safest option, but it may only shave a handful of registers and not cross the occupancy boundary. It also has to avoid collapsing back into the already-unsuccessful pair-compaction precheck under a different name.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if helper/lifetime cleanup leaves registers and active warps flat, because then the branch needs a stronger dataflow change rather than more structural cleanup.

directions.name: More direct register-packed pair export beyond shared float scratch
directions.hypothesis: Round 4 proved that paired export is on the right line, but the hot-band PTX branch still routes each winning tile pair through shared float scratch before BF16 packing. A more aggressive continuation is to keep the same 64x384 compute body and unchanged 64x96 tail, but push the pair export further toward direct register-packed stores so export-side shared/LSU traffic shrinks again. That is still on the same PTX mainline, but it is riskier than dir_01 because it changes the output path more deeply while occupancy is still unresolved.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Export-side shared traffic and short-scoreboard pressure remain a secondary but still meaningful residual after paired export batching.
directions.risk: This is the highest-risk direction in the set: register-packed export can raise registers, complicate correctness, and still fail to move the main one-block occupancy guardrail. It is better suited as a follow-up if the safer state/dataflow steps keep paying off.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if direct register-packed export raises registers or correctness risk without a clear reduction in shared/LSU pressure.

notes: All three directions remain strictly inside the current 64x384 hot-band PTX microkernel mainline and keep the 64x96 tail untouched. Ranking is anchored on the fresh restored baseline run 20260419_142213_bf16_gemm_v1_9bdc160 of the accepted c9d030a code: barrier and mio are already low, while occupancy is still one block/SM and long-scoreboard is the more obvious residual stall. Known negative evidence is carried forward: explicit mma.sync half-panel compute regressed, and pair-compaction plus panelized B-load reorder prechecks did not beat the base. Because this is the start of a 20-round run, the recommendation favors a medium-risk, sustainable full-width PTX dataflow improvement rather than a larger rewrite.
